title: YouTube

infobox.value: [Online video platform](/wiki/Online_video_platform "Online video platform"), [social media platform](/wiki/Social_media_platform "Social media platform")
901 Cherry Avenue [San Bruno, California](/wiki/San_Bruno,_California "San Bruno, California"), United States
* [Chad Hurley](/wiki/Chad_Hurley "Chad Hurley") * [Jawed Karim](/wiki/Jawed_Karim "Jawed Karim") * [Steve Chen](/wiki/Steve_Chen "Steve Chen")

* [YouTube Kids](/wiki/YouTube_Kids "YouTube Kids") * [YouTube Music](/wiki/YouTube_Music "YouTube Music") * [YouTube Premium](/wiki/YouTube_Premium "YouTube Premium") * [YouTube Shorts](/wiki/YouTube_Shorts "YouTube Shorts") * [YouTube TV](/wiki/YouTube_TV "YouTube TV")
Optional   * Not required to watch most videos; required for certain tasks such as uploading videos, viewing flagged (18+) videos, creating playlists, liking or disliking videos, and posting comments
Uploader holds copyright (standard license); [Creative Commons](/wiki/Creative_Commons "Creative Commons") can be selected.

is an American [online video sharing](/wiki/Online_video_platform "Online video platform") platform owned by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"). YouTube was founded on February 14, 2005, by [Chad Hurley](/wiki/Chad_Hurley "Chad Hurley"), [Jawed Karim](/wiki/Jawed_Karim "Jawed Karim"), and [Steve Chen](/wiki/Steve_Chen "Steve Chen"), who were former employees of [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Headquartered in [San Bruno, California](/wiki/San_Bruno,_California "San Bruno, California"), it is the [second-most-visited website](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_websites "List of most-visited websites") in the world, after [Google](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search") itself. In January 2024, YouTube had reached more than 2.7 billion [monthly active users](/wiki/Active_users "Active users"), who collectively consumed more than one billion hours of video content every day.[[8]](#cite_note-1_billion_hours-8) As of May 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), videos were being uploaded to the platform at a rate of more than 500 hours of [content](/wiki/Content_(media) "Content (media)") per minute,[[9]](#cite_note-500_hours_per_minute-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) and as of mid-2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), there were approximately 14.8 billion videos in total.[[11]](#cite_note-Germain-2025-11)

**YouTube** is an American [online video sharing](/wiki/Online_video_platform "Online video platform") platform owned by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"). YouTube was founded on February 14, 2005, by [Chad Hurley](/wiki/Chad_Hurley "Chad Hurley"), [Jawed Karim](/wiki/Jawed_Karim "Jawed Karim"), and [Steve Chen](/wiki/Steve_Chen "Steve Chen"), who were former employees of [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Headquartered in [San Bruno, California](/wiki/San_Bruno,_California "San Bruno, California"), it is the [second-most-visited website](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_websites "List of most-visited websites") in the world, after [Google](/wiki/Google_Search "Google Search") itself. In January 2024, YouTube had reached more than 2.7 billion [monthly active users](/wiki/Active_users "Active users"), who collectively consumed more than one billion hours of video content every day.[[8]](#cite_note-1_billion_hours-8) As of May 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), videos were being uploaded to the platform at a rate of more than 500 hours of [content](/wiki/Content_(media) "Content (media)") per minute,[[9]](#cite_note-500_hours_per_minute-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) and as of mid-2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), there were approximately 14.8 billion videos in total.[[11]](#cite_note-Germain-2025-11)

On November 13, 2006, YouTube was purchased by Google for US$1.65 billion (equivalent to $2.44 billion in 2025).[[12]](#cite_note-12) Google expanded YouTube's business model from generating revenue through [advertisements](/wiki/Advertising "Advertising") alone to offering paid content such as [movies](/wiki/YouTube_TV "YouTube TV") and [exclusive content](/wiki/List_of_YouTube_Premium_original_programming "List of YouTube Premium original programming") explicitly produced for YouTube. It also offers [YouTube Premium](/wiki/YouTube_Premium "YouTube Premium"), a paid subscription option for watching content without ads. YouTube incorporated the [Google AdSense](/wiki/Google_AdSense "Google AdSense") program, generating more revenue for both YouTube and approved content creators. In 2023, YouTube's advertising revenue totaled $31.7 billion, a 2% increase from the $31.1 billion reported in 2022.[[13]](#cite_note-13) From Q4 2023 to Q3 2024, YouTube's combined revenue from advertising and subscriptions exceeded $50 billion.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Since its purchase by Google, YouTube has expanded beyond the core website, creating [mobile apps](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app"), [network television](/wiki/Television_broadcaster "Television broadcaster"), [games](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game"), and the [ability to link](/wiki/Interoperability "Interoperability") with other platforms. Video categories on YouTube include [music videos](/wiki/Music_video "Music video"), [video clips](/wiki/Digital_video "Digital video"), [news](/wiki/News "News"), [short](/wiki/Short_film "Short film") and [feature films](/wiki/Feature_film "Feature film"), [songs](/wiki/Songs "Songs"), [documentaries](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film"), [movie trailers](/wiki/Trailer_(promotion) "Trailer (promotion)"), [teasers](/wiki/Teaser_(trailer) "Teaser (trailer)"), [TV spots](/wiki/Television_advertisement "Television advertisement"), [live streams](/wiki/Live_stream "Live stream"), [vlogs](/wiki/Vlog "Vlog"), and more. Most content is [generated by individuals](/wiki/User-generated_content "User-generated content"), including collaborations between [YouTubers](/wiki/YouTuber "YouTuber") and corporate [sponsors](/wiki/Sponsor_(commercial) "Sponsor (commercial)"). Established media, news, and entertainment corporations have also created and expanded their visibility on YouTube channels to reach bigger audiences.

YouTube has had [unprecedented social impact](/wiki/Social_impact_of_YouTube "Social impact of YouTube"), influencing [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"), [internet trends](/wiki/Internet_trend "Internet trend"), and creating multimillionaire celebrities. Despite its growth and success, the platform has been criticized for its facilitation of the spread of [misinformation](/wiki/Misinformation "Misinformation") and [copyrighted content](/wiki/YouTube_copyright_issues "YouTube copyright issues"), [routinely violating its users' privacy](/wiki/YouTube_and_privacy "YouTube and privacy"), [excessive censorship](/wiki/Censorship_by_YouTube "Censorship by YouTube"), endangering [the safety of children and their well-being](/wiki/Child_protection "Child protection"), and for its inconsistent implementation of platform guidelines.

[![Steve Chen](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Steve_Chen_%282022%29_02.png/120px-Steve_Chen_%282022%29_02.png)](/wiki/File:Steve_Chen_(2022)_02.png)

[Steve Chen](/wiki/Steve_Chen "Steve Chen")

[![Jawed Karim](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Jawed_Karim_2008.jpg/120px-Jawed_Karim_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jawed_Karim_2008.jpg)

[Jawed Karim](/wiki/Jawed_Karim "Jawed Karim")

YouTube was founded by Chad Hurley, Jawed Karim, and Steve Chen. The three were early employees at [PayPal](/wiki/PayPal "PayPal") and had become wealthy after [eBay](/wiki/EBay "EBay")'s acquisition of the company.[[15]](#cite_note-Jackpot-15) Hurley had studied design at the [Indiana University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Indiana_University_of_Pennsylvania "Indiana University of Pennsylvania"), and Chen and Karim studied [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science") together at the [University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

According to a story that has often been repeated in the media, Hurley and Chen developed the idea for YouTube during the early months of 2005, after they had experienced difficulty sharing videos that had been shot at a dinner party at Chen's apartment in [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). Karim did not attend the party and denied that it had occurred, but Chen remarked that the idea that YouTube was founded after a dinner party "was probably very strengthened by marketing ideas around creating a story that was very digestible".[[17]](#cite_note-YouTube_gurus-17)

Karim said the inspiration for YouTube came from the [Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVIII_halftime_show_controversy "Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy") when [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson")'s breast was briefly exposed by [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake") during the halftime show. Karim could not easily find video clips of the incident and the [2004 Indian Ocean tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami") online, which led to the idea of a video-sharing site.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) Hurley and Chen said that the original idea for YouTube was a video version of an [online dating service](/wiki/Online_dating_service "Online dating service") and had been influenced by the website [Hot or Not](/wiki/Hot_or_Not "Hot or Not").[[17]](#cite_note-YouTube_gurus-17)[[20]](#cite_note-20) They created posts on [Craigslist](/wiki/Craigslist "Craigslist") asking attractive women to upload videos of themselves to YouTube in exchange for a $100 reward.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Difficulty in finding enough dating videos led to a change of plans, with the site's founders deciding to allow all types of videos.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Youtube_logo.jpg/250px-Youtube_logo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Youtube_logo.jpg)

The YouTube logo used from its launch until 2007. It returned in 2008 before being removed again in 2010. Another version without "Broadcast Yourself" was used until 2011.

YouTube began as a [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital")–funded technology [startup](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company"). Between November 2005 and April 2006, the company raised money from various investors, with [Sequoia Capital](/wiki/Sequoia_Capital "Sequoia Capital") and Artis Capital Management being the largest two.[[15]](#cite_note-Jackpot-15)[[23]](#cite_note-23) YouTube's early headquarters were situated above a pizzeria and a Japanese restaurant in [San Mateo](/wiki/San_Mateo,_California "San Mateo, California"), California.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In February 2005, the company registered `www.youtube.com`.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The first video was uploaded on April 23, 2005. Titled "[Me at the zoo](/wiki/Me_at_the_zoo "Me at the zoo")", it shows co-founder Jawed Karim at the [San Diego Zoo](/wiki/San_Diego_Zoo "San Diego Zoo") and can still be viewed on the site.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) The same day, the company launched a public [beta](/wiki/Software_release_life_cycle "Software release life cycle") and by November, a [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") ad featuring [Ronaldinho](/wiki/Ronaldinho "Ronaldinho") became the first video to reach one million total views.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) The site exited beta in December 2005, by which time the site was receiving 8 million views a day.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) Clips at the time were limited to 100 megabytes, as little as 30 seconds of footage.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

YouTube was not the first video-sharing site on the Internet; [Vimeo](/wiki/Vimeo "Vimeo") was founded in November 2004, though that site remained a side project of its developers from [CollegeHumor](/wiki/CollegeHumor "CollegeHumor").[[33]](#cite_note-fortune_vimeo-33) On December 17, 2005, the same week YouTube exited beta, NBCUniversal *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* ran a sketch "[Lazy Sunday](/wiki/Lazy_Sunday_(The_Lonely_Island_song) "Lazy Sunday (The Lonely Island song)")" by [The Lonely Island](/wiki/The_Lonely_Island "The Lonely Island"). Besides helping to bolster ratings and long-term viewership for *Saturday Night Live*, "Lazy Sunday"'s status as an early [viral video](/wiki/Viral_video "Viral video") helped establish YouTube as an important website.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Unofficial uploads of the skit to YouTube drew in more than five million collective views by February 2006 before they were removed when [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal") requested it two months later based on copyright concerns.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Despite eventually being taken down, these duplicate uploads of the skit helped popularize YouTube's reach and led to the upload of more third-party content.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) The site grew rapidly; in July 2006, the company announced that more than 65,000 new videos were being uploaded every day and that the site was receiving 100 million video views per day.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The choice of the name `youtube.com` led to problems for a similarly named website, `utube.com`. That site's owner, [Universal Tube & Rollform Equipment](/wiki/Universal_Tube_%26_Rollform_Equipment "Universal Tube & Rollform Equipment") (Universal Tube), filed a lawsuit against YouTube in November 2006, after being regularly overloaded by people looking for YouTube. Universal Tube subsequently changed its website to `www.utubeonline.com`.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### "Broadcast Yourself" era (2006–2013)

On October 9, 2006, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") announced that they had acquired YouTube for $1.65 billion in Google stock.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) The deal was finalized on November 13, 2006.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Google's acquisition launched newfound interest in video-sharing sites; [IAC](/wiki/IAC_(company) "IAC (company)"), which now owned [Vimeo](/wiki/Vimeo "Vimeo"), focused on supporting the content creators to distinguish itself from YouTube.[[33]](#cite_note-fortune_vimeo-33) It was at this time that YouTube adopted the slogan "Broadcast Yourself".
The company experienced rapid growth. *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* wrote that in 2007, YouTube consumed as much [bandwidth](/wiki/Bandwidth_(computing) "Bandwidth (computing)") as the entire Internet in 2000.[[46]](#cite_note-46) By 2010, the company had reached a [market share](/wiki/Market_share "Market share") of around 43% and more than 14 billion views of videos, according to [comScore](/wiki/ComScore "ComScore").[[47]](#cite_note-47) That year, the company simplified its interface to increase the time users would spend on the site.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In 2011, more than three billion videos were being watched each day with 48 hours of new videos uploaded every minute.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) Most of these views came from a relatively small number of videos; according to a software engineer at that time, 30% of videos accounted for 99% of views on the site.[[52]](#cite_note-52) That year, the company again changed its interface and at the same time, introduced a new logo with a darker shade of red.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) A subsequent interface change, designed to unify the experience across desktop, TV, and mobile, was rolled out in 2013.[[55]](#cite_note-55) By that point, more than 100 hours were being uploaded every minute, increasing to 300 hours by November 2014.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/901_Cherry_Avenue.jpg/250px-901_Cherry_Avenue.jpg)](/wiki/File:901_Cherry_Avenue.jpg)

YouTube's headquarters in [San Bruno, California](/wiki/San_Bruno,_California "San Bruno, California"), April 2017

During that time, the company also went through some organizational changes. In October 2006, YouTube moved to a new office in [San Bruno, California](/wiki/San_Bruno,_California "San Bruno, California").[[58]](#cite_note-58) Hurley announced that he would be stepping down as chief executive officer of YouTube to take an advisory role and that [Salar Kamangar](/wiki/Salar_Kamangar "Salar Kamangar") would take over as head of the company in October 2010.[[59]](#cite_note-59) In April 2009, YouTube partnered with [Vevo](/wiki/Vevo "Vevo").[[60]](#cite_note-60) In April 2010, [Lady Gaga](/wiki/Lady_Gaga "Lady Gaga")'s "[Bad Romance](/wiki/Bad_Romance "Bad Romance")" became the [most-viewed video](/wiki/List_of_most-viewed_YouTube_videos "List of most-viewed YouTube videos"), becoming the first video to reach 200 million views on May 9, 2010.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

YouTube [faced a major lawsuit](/wiki/Viacom_International_Inc._v._YouTube,_Inc. "Viacom International Inc. v. YouTube, Inc.") by [Viacom International](/wiki/Viacom_(2005%E2%80%932019) "Viacom (2005–2019)") in 2011 that nearly resulted in the discontinuation of the website. The lawsuit was filed due to alleged [copyright infringement](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") of Viacom's material by YouTube. However, the [United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit") ruled that YouTube was not liable, and thus, YouTube won the case in 2012.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Susan Wojcicki's leadership (2014–2023)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Logo_of_YouTube_%282015-2017%29.svg/250px-Logo_of_YouTube_%282015-2017%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Logo_of_YouTube_(2015-2017).svg)

YouTube logo from 2015 until 2017

[Susan Wojcicki](/wiki/Susan_Wojcicki "Susan Wojcicki") was appointed [CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") of YouTube in February 2014.[[63]](#cite_note-63) In January 2016, YouTube expanded its headquarters in [San Bruno](/wiki/San_Bruno,_California "San Bruno, California") by purchasing an office park for $215 million. The complex has 51,468 square metres (554,000 square feet) of space and can house up to 2,800 employees.[[64]](#cite_note-64) YouTube officially launched the "polymer" redesign of its user interfaces based on [Material Design](/wiki/Material_Design "Material Design") language as its default, as well as a redesigned logo that is built around the service's play button emblem in August 2017.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Through this period, YouTube tried several new ways to generate revenue beyond advertisements. In 2013, YouTube launched a pilot program for content providers to offer premium, subscription-based channels.[[66]](#cite_note-subscription-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) This effort was discontinued in January 2018 and relaunched in June, with US$4.99 channel subscriptions.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) These channel subscriptions complemented the existing Super Chat ability, launched in 2017, which allows viewers to donate between $1 and $500 to have their comment highlighted.[[70]](#cite_note-70) In 2014, YouTube announced a subscription service known as "Music Key", which bundled ad-free streaming of music content on YouTube with the existing [Google Play Music](/wiki/Google_Play_Music "Google Play Music") service.[[71]](#cite_note-Newton-71) The service continued to evolve in 2015 when YouTube announced [YouTube Red](/wiki/YouTube_Red "YouTube Red"), a new premium service that would offer ad-free access to all content on the platform (succeeding the Music Key service released the previous year), premium original series, and films produced by YouTube personalities, as well as background playback of content on mobile devices. YouTube also released [YouTube Music](/wiki/YouTube_Music "YouTube Music"), a third app oriented towards streaming and discovering the music content hosted on the YouTube platform.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Popper-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)

The company also attempted to create products appealing to specific viewers. YouTube released a mobile app known as [YouTube Kids](/wiki/YouTube_Kids "YouTube Kids") in 2015, which was designed to provide an experience optimized for children. It features a simplified user interface, curated selections of channels featuring age-appropriate content, and parental control features.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Also in 2015, YouTube launched YouTube Gaming—a [video gaming](/wiki/Video_gaming "Video gaming")-oriented vertical and app for videos and live streaming, intended to compete with the [Amazon.com](/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com")-owned [Twitch](/wiki/Twitch_(service) "Twitch (service)").[[76]](#cite_note-76) In April 2018, [a shooting](/wiki/2018_YouTube_headquarters_shooting "2018 YouTube headquarters shooting") occurred at YouTube's headquarters in San Bruno, California, which wounded four and resulted in the death of the shooter.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

By February 2017, one billion hours of YouTube videos were being watched every day, and 400 hours worth of videos were uploaded every minute.[[8]](#cite_note-1_billion_hours-8)[[78]](#cite_note-78) Two years later, the uploads had risen to more than 500 hours per minute.[[9]](#cite_note-500_hours_per_minute-9) During the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), when most of the world was under [stay-at-home orders](/wiki/Stay-at-home_order "Stay-at-home order"), usage of services like YouTube significantly increased. [Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes") estimated that YouTube accounted for 16% of all [internet traffic](/wiki/Internet_traffic "Internet traffic"), as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), up from 11% in 2018, before the pandemic.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) In response to EU officials requesting that such services reduce bandwidth to make sure medical entities had sufficient bandwidth to share information, YouTube and [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") said they would reduce streaming quality for at least thirty days as to cut bandwidth use of their services by 25% to comply with the EU's request.[[81]](#cite_note-81) YouTube later announced that they would continue with this move worldwide: "We continue to work closely with governments and network operators around the globe to do our part to minimize stress on the system during this unprecedented situation."[[82]](#cite_note-82)

After a 2018 complaint alleging violations of the [Children's Online Privacy Protection Act](/wiki/Children%27s_Online_Privacy_Protection_Act "Children's Online Privacy Protection Act") (COPPA),[[83]](#cite_note-83) the company was fined $170 million by the FTC for collecting personal information from minors under the age of 13.[[84]](#cite_note-84) YouTube was also ordered to create systems to increase children's privacy.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) Following criticisms of its implementation of those systems, YouTube started treating all videos designated as "made for kids" as liable under COPPA on January 6, 2020.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) Joining the [YouTube Kids](/wiki/YouTube_Kids "YouTube Kids") app, the company created a supervised mode, designed more for [tweens](/wiki/Preadolescent "Preadolescent"), in 2021.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Additionally, to compete with [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok") and [Instagram Reels](/wiki/Instagram_Reels "Instagram Reels"), YouTube released [YouTube Shorts](/wiki/YouTube_Shorts "YouTube Shorts"), a short-form video platform.[[90]](#cite_note-90) During that period, YouTube entered disputes with other tech companies. For over a year, in 2018–19, no YouTube app was available for [Amazon Fire](/wiki/Amazon_Fire_TV "Amazon Fire TV") products.[[91]](#cite_note-91) In 2020, [Roku](/wiki/Roku "Roku") removed the YouTube TV app from its streaming store after the two companies were unable to reach an agreement.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

After testing earlier in 2021, YouTube removed public display of dislike counts on videos in November 2021, claiming the reason for the removal was, based on its internal research, that users often used the dislike feature as a form of [cyberbullying](/wiki/Cyberbullying "Cyberbullying") and [brigading](/wiki/Vote_brigading "Vote brigading").[[93]](#cite_note-93) While some users praised the move as a way to discourage [trolls](/wiki/Internet_troll "Internet troll"), others felt that hiding dislikes would make it harder for viewers to recognize [clickbait](/wiki/Clickbait "Clickbait") or unhelpful videos and that other features already existed for creators to limit bullying. YouTube co-founder [Jawed Karim](/wiki/Jawed_Karim "Jawed Karim") referred to the update as "a stupid idea" and said that the real reason behind the change was "not a good one, and not one that will be publicly disclosed." He felt that users' ability on a social platform to identify harmful content was essential, saying, "The process works, and there's a name for it: the [wisdom of the crowds](/wiki/Wisdom_of_the_crowd "Wisdom of the crowd"). The process breaks when the platform interferes with it. Then, the platform invariably declines."[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) Shortly after the announcement, software developer Dmitry Selivanov created Return YouTube Dislike, an [open-source](/wiki/Open_source "Open source"), third-party [browser extension](/wiki/Browser_extension "Browser extension") for [Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome") and [Firefox](/wiki/Firefox "Firefox") that allows users to see a video's number of dislikes.[[97]](#cite_note-97) In a letter published on January 25, 2022, by then YouTube CEO [Susan Wojcicki](/wiki/Susan_Wojcicki "Susan Wojcicki"), acknowledged that removing public dislike counts was a controversial decision, but reiterated that she stands by this decision, claiming that "it reduced dislike attacks."[[98]](#cite_note-98)

In 2022, YouTube launched an experiment where the company would show users who watched longer videos on TVs a long chain of short unskippable adverts, intending to consolidate all ads into the beginning of a video. Following public outrage over the unprecedented amount of unskippable ads, YouTube "ended" the experiment on September 19 of the same year.[[99]](#cite_note-99) In October, YouTube announced that they would be rolling out customizable user handles in addition to channel names, which would also become channel URLs.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Neal Mohan leadership (2023–present)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/YouTube_Logo_2017.svg/250px-YouTube_Logo_2017.svg.png)](/wiki/File:YouTube_Logo_2017.svg)

YouTube logo from August 2017 until February 2025

On February 16, 2023, Wojcicki announced that she would step down as CEO, with [Neal Mohan](/wiki/Neal_Mohan "Neal Mohan") named as her successor. Wojcicki took on an advisory role for Google and parent company [Alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.").[[101]](#cite_note-Wojcicki_steps_down-101) Wojcicki died a year and a half later from [non-small-cell lung cancer](/wiki/Non-small-cell_lung_cancer "Non-small-cell lung cancer"), on August 9, 2024.[[102]](#cite_note-102) In late October 2023, YouTube began cracking down on the use of [ad blockers](/wiki/Ad_blocker "Ad blocker") on the platform. Users of ad blockers may be given a pop-up warning saying "Video player will be blocked after 3 videos". Users of ad blockers are shown a message asking them to allow ads or inviting them to subscribe to the ad-free [YouTube Premium](/wiki/YouTube_Premium "YouTube Premium") subscription plan. YouTube says that the use of ad blockers violates its terms of service.[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) In April 2024, YouTube announced it would be "strengthening our enforcement on third-party apps that violate YouTube's Terms of Service, specifically ad-blocking apps".[[105]](#cite_note-105) Starting in June 2024, [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome "Google Chrome") announced that it would be replacing Manifest V2 in favor of [Manifest V3](/wiki/Manifest_V3 "Manifest V3"), effectively killing support for most ad-blockers.[[106]](#cite_note-106) Around the same time, YouTube started using server-side ad injection, which allows the platform to inject the ads directly into the video, instead of having the ad as a separate file which can be blocked.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

In September 2023, YouTube announced an in-app gaming platform called Playables. It was made accessible to all users in May 2024, expanding from an initial offering limited to premium subscribers. In December 2024, YouTube began testing a new multiplayer feature for that service, supporting multiplayer functionality across desktop and mobile devices. As of December 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit) the Playables catalog has over 130 games in various genres, including [trivia](/wiki/Trivia_game "Trivia game"), action, and sports.[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109) In December 2024, YouTube introduced new guidelines prohibiting videos with clickbait titles to enhance content quality and combat misinformation. The platform aims to penalize creators using misleading or sensationalized titles, with potential actions including video removal or channel suspension.[[110]](#cite_note-110) According to YouTube, this guideline will gradually roll out in India first, but will expand to more countries in the coming months.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

On February 14, 2025, YouTube celebrated 20 years since its founding.[[112]](#cite_note-112) On July 30, 2025, amid the implementation of the [Online Safety Act 2023](/wiki/Online_Safety_Act_2023 "Online Safety Act 2023") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), Google announced that it would begin to enforce "[age assurance](/wiki/Age_verification_system "Age verification system")" policies for selected users in the United States as a trial. [Machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") will be used to determine the age of the user (regardless of any account information indicating their age) and restrict access to certain content and features across all Google properties, including YouTube (including, in particular, disabling personalized advertising and enabling certain digital wellbeing limits), if they are assumed to be under 18. On YouTube, this will be based on factors such as searches and video history, and the age of the account. The user must go through age verification via payment, scanned ID, or selfie to access all features if they are detected to be a minor.[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) On April 9, 2025, YouTube expressed support for the NO FAKES Act of 2025, introduced by Senator [Chris Coons](/wiki/Chris_Coons "Chris Coons") (D-DE) and Senator [Marsha Blackburn](/wiki/Marsha_Blackburn "Marsha Blackburn") (R-TN), and announced an expansion of its pilot program that is designed to identify content generated by AI.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

On September 23, 2025, YouTube parent company Alphabet announced that it would reinstate creators who were banned for spreading [misinformation about COVID-19](/wiki/Misinformation_about_COVID-19 "Misinformation about COVID-19") and the [2020 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2020_U.S._presidential_election "2020 U.S. presidential election").[[116]](#cite_note-116) Within the context of the [suspension of Jimmy Kimmel](/wiki/Suspension_of_Jimmy_Kimmel "Suspension of Jimmy Kimmel") and debates about [free speech in the United States](/wiki/Free_speech_in_the_United_States "Free speech in the United States"), Vice President [JD Vance](/wiki/JD_Vance "JD Vance") defended Kimmel's suspension and instead cited a letter sent by Alphabet legal counsel Daniel F. Donovan to [U.S. House Judiciary Committee](/wiki/U.S._House_Judiciary_Committee "U.S. House Judiciary Committee") chairman [Jim Jordan](/wiki/Jim_Jordan "Jim Jordan"), claiming the [Biden administration](/wiki/Biden_administration "Biden administration") pressured YouTube to remove "non-violative user-generated content" containing misinformation about the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 election, and announced that it would reinstate content creators previously banned due to the cited content;[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118) however, such claims have not formally been proven, and the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") dismissed in 2024 the [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment "First Amendment") case *[Murthy v. Missouri](/wiki/Murthy_v._Missouri "Murthy v. Missouri")* (which claimed the Biden administration had pressured social media companies to censor conservative views, government criticism, and COVID-19 misinformation), ruling 6–3 that neither the attorneys general of Missouri and Louisiana nor other respondents had standing to bring the case. The decision of Alphabet to bring back YouTube creators who engaged in [misinformation](/wiki/Misinformation "Misinformation") was criticized for prioritizing "free expression" over "facts".[[119]](#cite_note-119)

## Senior leadership

YouTube has been led by a CEO since its founding in 2005, beginning with [Chad Hurley](/wiki/Chad_Hurley "Chad Hurley"), who led the company until 2010. After Google acquired YouTube, the CEO role was retained. [Salar Kamangar](/wiki/Salar_Kamangar "Salar Kamangar") took over Hurley's position and kept the job until 2014. He was replaced by [Susan Wojcicki](/wiki/Susan_Wojcicki "Susan Wojcicki"), who later resigned in 2023.[[101]](#cite_note-Wojcicki_steps_down-101) On February 16, 2023, [Neal Mohan](/wiki/Neal_Mohan "Neal Mohan") was appointed as the new CEO.[[101]](#cite_note-Wojcicki_steps_down-101)

## Features

Main article: [List of YouTube features](/wiki/List_of_YouTube_features "List of YouTube features")

YouTube offers different features based on user verification, such as standard or basic features like uploading videos, creating playlists, and using [YouTube Music](/wiki/YouTube_Music "YouTube Music"), with limits based on daily activity (verification via phone number or channel history increases feature availability and daily usage limits); intermediate or additional features like longer videos (over 15 minutes), live streaming, custom thumbnails, and creating podcasts; advanced features like content ID appeals, embedding live streams, applying for monetization, clickable links, adding chapters, and pinning comments on videos or posts.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

## Videos

Main article: [List of YouTube videos](/wiki/List_of_YouTube_videos "List of YouTube videos")

In January 2012, it was estimated that visitors to YouTube spent an average of 15 minutes a day on the site, in contrast to the four or five hours a day spent by a typical US citizen watching television.[[121]](#cite_note-121) In 2017, viewers on average watched YouTube on mobile devices for more than an hour every day.[[122]](#cite_note-122) In December 2012, two billion views were removed from the view counts of Universal and [Sony](/wiki/Sony_Music_Entertainment "Sony Music Entertainment") music videos on YouTube, prompting a claim by *[The Daily Dot](/wiki/The_Daily_Dot "The Daily Dot")* that the views had been deleted due to a violation of the site's terms of service, which ban the use of automated processes to inflate view counts. That was disputed by *Billboard*, which said that the two billion views had been moved to Vevo, since the videos were no longer active on YouTube.[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

On August 5, 2015, YouTube patched the formerly notorious behavior, which caused a video's [view count](/wiki/Views_(social_media) "Views (social media)") to freeze at "301" (later "301+") until the actual count was verified to prevent [view count fraud](/wiki/Click_fraud "Click fraud").[[125]](#cite_note-125) YouTube view counts again began updating in real time.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Since September 2019, subscriber counts are abbreviated. Only three leading digits of channels' subscriber counts are indicated publicly, compromising the function of third-party real-time indicators such as [Social Blade](/wiki/Social_Blade "Social Blade"). Exact counts remain available to channel operators inside YouTube Studio.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

On November 11, 2021, after testing out this change in March of the same year, YouTube announced it would start hiding dislike counts on videos, making them invisible to viewers. The company stated the decision was in response to experiments which confirmed that smaller YouTube creators were more likely to be targeted in dislike [brigading](/wiki/Vote_brigading "Vote brigading") and harassment. Creators will still be able to see the number of likes and dislikes in the YouTube Studio dashboard tool, according to YouTube.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) YouTube has an estimated 14.8 billion videos with about 4% of those never having a view.[[11]](#cite_note-Germain-2025-11) Just over 85% have fewer than 1,000 views.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

### Copyright issues

Main article: [YouTube copyright issues](/wiki/YouTube_copyright_issues "YouTube copyright issues")

Further information: [§ Revenue to copyright holders](#Revenue_to_copyright_holders)

YouTube has faced numerous challenges and criticisms in its attempts to deal with copyright, including the site's first viral video, [Lazy Sunday](/wiki/Lazy_Sunday_(The_Lonely_Island_song) "Lazy Sunday (The Lonely Island song)"), which had to be taken down due to copyright concerns.[[132]](#cite_note-132) At the time of uploading a video, YouTube users are shown a message asking them not to violate copyright laws.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Despite this advice, many unauthorized clips of copyrighted material remain on YouTube. YouTube does not view videos before they are posted online, and it is left to copyright holders to issue a [DMCA](/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act "Digital Millennium Copyright Act") [takedown notice](/wiki/Takedown_notice "Takedown notice") pursuant to the terms of the [Online Copyright Infringement Liability Limitation Act](/wiki/Online_Copyright_Infringement_Liability_Limitation_Act "Online Copyright Infringement Liability Limitation Act"). Any successful complaint about copyright infringement results in a [YouTube copyright strike](/wiki/YouTube_copyright_strike "YouTube copyright strike"). Three successful complaints for [copyright infringement](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") against a user account will result in the account and all of its uploaded videos being deleted.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) From 2007 to 2009 organizations including [Viacom](/wiki/Viacom_(2005%E2%80%932019) "Viacom (2005–2019)"), [Mediaset](/wiki/Mediaset "Mediaset"), and the English [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") have filed lawsuits against YouTube, claiming that it has done too little to prevent the uploading of copyrighted material.[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

In August 2008, a US court ruled in *[Lenz v. Universal Music Corp.](/wiki/Lenz_v._Universal_Music_Corp. "Lenz v. Universal Music Corp.")* that copyright holders cannot order the removal of an online file without first determining whether the posting reflected [fair use](/wiki/Fair_use "Fair use") of the material.[[139]](#cite_note-139) YouTube's owner Google announced in November 2015 that they would help cover the legal cost in select cases where they believe fair use defenses apply.[[140]](#cite_note-140) In the 2011 case of *[Smith v. Summit Entertainment LLC](/wiki/Smith_v._Summit_Entertainment_LLC "Smith v. Summit Entertainment LLC")*, professional singer Matt Smith sued Summit Entertainment for the wrongful use of copyright takedown notices on YouTube.[[141]](#cite_note-141) He asserted seven [causes of action](/wiki/Causes_of_action "Causes of action"), and four were ruled in Smith's favor.[[142]](#cite_note-142) In April 2012, a court in Hamburg ruled that YouTube could be held responsible for copyrighted material posted by its users.[[143]](#cite_note-143) On November 1, 2016, the dispute with GEMA was resolved, with Google content ID being used to allow advertisements to be added to videos with content protected by GEMA.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

In April 2013, it was reported that [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") and YouTube have a contractual agreement that prevents content blocked on YouTube by a request from UMG from being restored, even if the uploader of the video files a DMCA counter-notice.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) As part of YouTube Music, Universal and YouTube signed an agreement in 2017, which was followed by separate agreements other major labels, which gave the company the right to advertising revenue when its music was played on YouTube.[[147]](#cite_note-147) By 2019, creators were having videos taken down or demonetized when Content ID identified even short segments of copyrighted music within a much longer video, with different levels of enforcement depending on the record label.[[148]](#cite_note-fighting-148) Experts noted that some of these clips said qualified for fair use.[[148]](#cite_note-fighting-148)

#### Content ID

Main article: [Content ID](/wiki/Content_ID "Content ID")

In June 2007, YouTube began trials of a system for automatic detection of uploaded videos that infringe copyright. Google CEO Eric Schmidt regarded this system as necessary for resolving lawsuits such as the one from [Viacom](/wiki/Viacom_(2005%E2%80%932019) "Viacom (2005–2019)"), which alleged that YouTube profited from content that it did not have the right to distribute.[[149]](#cite_note-149) The system, which was initially called "Video Identification"[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151) and later became known as Content ID,[[152]](#cite_note-152) creates an ID File for copyrighted audio and video material, and stores it in a database. When a video is uploaded, it is checked against the database, and flags the video as a copyright violation if a match is found.[[153]](#cite_note-153) When this occurs, the content owner has the choice of blocking the video to make it unviewable, tracking the viewing statistics of the video, or adding advertisements to the video.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An independent test in 2009 uploaded multiple versions of the same song to YouTube and concluded that while the system was "surprisingly resilient" in finding copyright violations in the audio tracks of videos, it was not infallible.[[154]](#cite_note-154) The use of Content ID to remove material automatically has led to [controversy](/wiki/YouTube_copyright_issues "YouTube copyright issues") in some cases, as the videos have not been checked by a human for fair use.[[155]](#cite_note-155) If a YouTube user disagrees with a decision by Content ID, it is possible to fill in a form disputing the decision.[[156]](#cite_note-156) Before 2016, videos were not monetized until the dispute was resolved. Since April 2016, videos continue to be monetized while the dispute is in progress, and the money goes to whoever won the dispute.[[157]](#cite_note-157) Should the uploader want to monetize the video again, they may remove the disputed audio in the "Video Manager".[[158]](#cite_note-158) YouTube has cited the effectiveness of Content ID as one of the reasons why the site's rules were modified in December 2010 to allow some users to upload videos of unlimited length.[[159]](#cite_note-159)

### Russia

In 2021, two accounts linked to [RT DE](/wiki/RT_DE "RT DE"), the German channel of the Russian state-owned [RT](/wiki/RT_(TV_network) "RT (TV network)") network, were removed for breaching YouTube's policies relating to COVID-19.[[160]](#cite_note-160) Russia threatened to ban YouTube after the platform deleted two German RT channels in September 2021.[[161]](#cite_note-161) Shortly after the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine") in 2022, YouTube removed all channels funded by the Russian state.[[162]](#cite_note-162) YouTube expanded the removal of Russian content from its site to include channels described as 'pro-Russian'. In June 2022, the *War Gonzo* channel run by Russian military blogger and journalist [Semyon Pegov](/wiki/Semyon_Pegov "Semyon Pegov") was deleted.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

In July 2023, YouTube removed the channel of British journalist [Graham Phillips](/wiki/Graham_Phillips_(journalist) "Graham Phillips (journalist)"), active in covering the [war in Donbas](/wiki/War_in_Donbas "War in Donbas") from 2014.[[164]](#cite_note-164) In August 2023, a Moscow court fined Google 3 million rubles, around $35,000, for not deleting what it said was "fake news about the war in Ukraine".[[165]](#cite_note-165) In October 2024, a Russian court fined Google 2 undecillion [rubles](/wiki/Russian_ruble "Russian ruble") (equivalent to US$20 decillion) for restricting Russian state media channels on YouTube.[[166]](#cite_note-166) State news agency [TASS](/wiki/TASS "TASS") reported that Google is allowed to return to the Russian market only if it complies with the court's decision.[[167]](#cite_note-167) Kremlin spokesperson [Dmitry Peskov](/wiki/Dmitry_Peskov "Dmitry Peskov") labeled the court decision as "symbolic" and warned Google that it "should not be restricting the actions of our broadcasters on its platform."[[168]](#cite_note-168)

### April Fools gags

See also: [List of Google April Fools' Day jokes](/wiki/List_of_Google_April_Fools%27_Day_jokes "List of Google April Fools' Day jokes")

YouTube featured an [April Fools](/wiki/April_Fools%27_Day "April Fools' Day") prank on the site on April 1 of every year from 2008 to 2016. In 2008, all links to videos on the main page were redirected to [Rick Astley](/wiki/Rick_Astley "Rick Astley")'s music video "[Never Gonna Give You Up](/wiki/Never_Gonna_Give_You_Up "Never Gonna Give You Up")", a prank known as "[rickrolling](/wiki/Rickrolling "Rickrolling")".[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170) The next year, when clicking on a video on the main page, the whole page turned upside down, which YouTube claimed was a "new layout".[[171]](#cite_note-171) In 2010, YouTube temporarily released a "TEXTp" mode which rendered video imagery into [ASCII art](/wiki/ASCII_art "ASCII art") letters "in order to reduce bandwidth costs by $1 per second."[[172]](#cite_note-172)

The next year, the site celebrated its "100th anniversary" with a range of sepia-toned silent, early 1900s-style films, including a parody of [Keyboard Cat](/wiki/Keyboard_Cat "Keyboard Cat").[[173]](#cite_note-173) In 2012, clicking on the image of a DVD next to the site logo led to a video about a purported option to order every YouTube video for home delivery on DVD.[[174]](#cite_note-174) In 2013, YouTube teamed up with satirical newspaper company *[The Onion](/wiki/The_Onion "The Onion")* to claim in an uploaded video that the video-sharing website was launched as a contest which had finally come to an end, and would shut down for ten years before being re-launched in 2023, featuring only the winning video. The video starred several [YouTube celebrities](/wiki/Internet_celebrity "Internet celebrity"), including [Antoine Dodson](/wiki/Antoine_Dodson "Antoine Dodson"). A video of two presenters announcing the nominated videos streamed live for 12 hours.[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176)

In 2014, YouTube announced that it was responsible for the creation of all viral video trends, and revealed previews of upcoming trends, such as "Clocking", "Kissing Dad", and "Glub Glub Water Dance".[[177]](#cite_note-177) The next year, YouTube added a music button to the video bar that played samples from "[Sandstorm](/wiki/Sandstorm_(instrumental) "Sandstorm (instrumental)")" by [Darude](/wiki/Darude "Darude").[[178]](#cite_note-178) In 2016, YouTube introduced an option to watch every video on the platform in [360-degree](/wiki/360-degree_video "360-degree video") mode with [Snoop Dogg](/wiki/Snoop_Dogg "Snoop Dogg").[[179]](#cite_note-179)

## Services

### YouTube Premium

Main article: [YouTube Premium](/wiki/YouTube_Premium "YouTube Premium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/YouTube_Premium_logo_2024.svg/250px-YouTube_Premium_logo_2024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:YouTube_Premium_logo_2024.svg)

Logo of YouTube Premium

[YouTube Premium](/wiki/YouTube_Premium "YouTube Premium") (formerly Music Key and YouTube Red) is YouTube's premium subscription service. It offers advertising-free streaming, access to [original programming](/wiki/YouTube_Originals "YouTube Originals"), and background and offline video playback on mobile devices.[[180]](#cite_note-180) YouTube Premium was originally announced on November 12, 2014, as "Music Key", a [subscription music streaming](/wiki/Comparison_of_on-demand_music_streaming_services "Comparison of on-demand music streaming services") service, and was intended to integrate with and replace the existing [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") Music "All Access" service.[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[71]](#cite_note-Newton-71)[[182]](#cite_note-182) On October 28, 2015, the service was relaunched as YouTube Red, offering ad-free streaming of all videos and access to exclusive original content.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184)[[73]](#cite_note-Popper-73) As of November 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), the service has 1.5 million subscribers, with a further million on a free-trial basis.[[185]](#cite_note-185) As of June 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), the first season of [YouTube Originals](/wiki/List_of_YouTube_Premium_original_programming "List of YouTube Premium original programming") had received 250 million views in total.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

### YouTube Kids

Main article: [YouTube Kids](/wiki/YouTube_Kids "YouTube Kids")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/YouTube_Kids_logo.svg/250px-YouTube_Kids_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:YouTube_Kids_logo.svg)

Logo of YouTube Kids

[YouTube Kids](/wiki/YouTube_Kids "YouTube Kids") is an American children's video app developed by YouTube, a subsidiary of [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"). The app was developed in response to parental and government scrutiny on the content available to children.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The app provides a version of the service oriented towards children, with curated selections of content, parental control features, and filtering of videos deemed inappropriate viewing for children aged under 13, 8 or 5, depending on the age grouping chosen. First released on February 15, 2015, as an [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") and [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") [mobile app](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app"), the app has since been released for [LG](/wiki/LG_Electronics "LG Electronics"), [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics"), and [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") [smart TVs](/wiki/Smart_TV "Smart TV"), as well as for [Android TV](/wiki/Android_TV "Android TV"). On May 27, 2020, it became available on [Apple TV](/wiki/Apple_TV_(device) "Apple TV (device)"). As of September 2019, the app is available in 69 countries, including Hong Kong and Macau, and one province. YouTube launched a web-based version of YouTube Kids on August 30, 2019.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

### YouTube Music

Main article: [YouTube Music](/wiki/YouTube_Music "YouTube Music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/YouTube_Music_2024.svg/250px-YouTube_Music_2024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:YouTube_Music_2024.svg)

Logo of YouTube Music

On September 28, 2016, YouTube named [Lyor Cohen](/wiki/Lyor_Cohen "Lyor Cohen"), the co-founder of [300 Entertainment](/wiki/300_Entertainment "300 Entertainment") and former [Warner Music Group](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") executive, the Global Head of Music.[[188]](#cite_note-188) In early 2018, Cohen began hinting at the possible launch of YouTube's new subscription music streaming service, a platform that would compete with other services such as [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") and [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music").[[189]](#cite_note-189) On May 22, 2018, the music streaming platform named "YouTube Music" was launched for people who mostly listen to music on YouTube.[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191)[[192]](#cite_note-192)

### YouTube Movies & TV

YouTube Movies & TV is a [video on demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand") (VOD) service that offers movies and television shows for purchase or rental, depending on availability, along with a selection of movies (encompassing between 100 and 500 titles overall) that are free to stream, with interspersed ad breaks. YouTube began offering free-to-view movie titles to its users in November 2018; selections of new movies are added and others removed, unannounced each month.[[193]](#cite_note-193) In March 2021, Google announced plans to gradually [deprecate](/wiki/Deprecate "Deprecate") the Google Play Movies & TV app, and eventually migrate all users to the YouTube app's Movies & TV store to view, rent and purchase movies and TV shows (first affecting Roku, Samsung, LG, and Vizio smart TV users on July 15).[[194]](#cite_note-194)[[195]](#cite_note-195) Google Play Movies & TV formally shut down on January 17, 2024, with the web version of that platform migrated to YouTube as an expansion of the Movies & TV store to desktop users. Other functions of Google Play Movies & TV were integrated into the [Google TV](/wiki/Google_TV_(service) "Google TV (service)") service.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

#### YouTube Primetime Channels

On November 1, 2022, YouTube launched Primetime Channels, a channel store platform offering third-party subscription streaming add-ons sold a la carte through the YouTube website and app, competing with similar subscription add-on stores operated by [Apple](/wiki/Apple_TV_app#Apple_TV_Channels "Apple TV app"), [Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video#Amazon_Channels "Amazon Prime Video") and [Roku](/wiki/The_Roku_Channel "The Roku Channel"). The add-ons can be purchased through the YouTube Movies & TV hub or through the official YouTube channels of the available services; subscribers of YouTube TV add-ons that are sold through Primetime Channels can also access their content via the YouTube app and website. A total of 34 streaming services (including [Paramount+](/wiki/Paramount%2B "Paramount+"), [Showtime](/wiki/Showtime_(TV_network) "Showtime (TV network)"), [Starz](/wiki/Starz "Starz"), [MGM+](/wiki/MGM%2B "MGM+"), [AMC+](/wiki/AMC%2B "AMC+") and [ViX+](/wiki/Vix_(streaming_service) "Vix (streaming service)")) were initially available for purchase.[[197]](#cite_note-197)[[198]](#cite_note-198)

[NFL Sunday Ticket](/wiki/NFL_Sunday_Ticket "NFL Sunday Ticket"), as part of a broader residential distribution deal with [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") signed in December 2022 that also made it available to YouTube TV subscribers, was added to Primetime Channels as a standalone add-on on [August 16, 2023](/wiki/2023_NFL_season "2023 NFL season").[[199]](#cite_note-199)[[200]](#cite_note-200) The ad-free tier of [Max](/wiki/Max_(streaming_service) "Max (streaming service)") was added to Primetime Channels on December 12, 2023, coinciding with YouTube TV converting its separate HBO (for base plan subscribers) and HBO Max (for all subscribers) linear/VOD add-ons into a single combined Max offering.[[201]](#cite_note-201)[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[note 1]](#cite_note-203)

### YouTube TV

Main article: [YouTube TV](/wiki/YouTube_TV "YouTube TV")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/YouTube_TV_logo.svg/250px-YouTube_TV_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:YouTube_TV_logo.svg)

Logo of YouTube TV

On February 28, 2017, in a press announcement held at YouTube Space Los Angeles, YouTube announced YouTube TV, an [over-the-top](/wiki/Over-the-top_media_service "Over-the-top media service") [MVPD](/wiki/Multichannel_video_programming_distributor "Multichannel video programming distributor")-style subscription service that would be available for United States customers for US$65 per month. Initially launching in five major markets ([New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco")) on April 5, 2017,[[203]](#cite_note-204)[[204]](#cite_note-205) the service offers live streams of programming from the five major broadcast networks ([ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW"), [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") and [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), along with selected [MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV") affiliates and [independent stations](/wiki/Independent_station "Independent station") in certain markets), as well as approximately 60 cable channels owned by companies such as [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company"), [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global"), [Fox Corporation](/wiki/Fox_Corporation "Fox Corporation"), [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal"), [Allen Media Group](/wiki/Allen_Media_Group "Allen Media Group") and [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery") (including among others [Bravo](/wiki/Bravo_(American_TV_network) "Bravo (American TV network)"), [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network"), [Syfy](/wiki/Syfy "Syfy"), [Disney Channel](/wiki/Disney_Channel "Disney Channel"), [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"), [Cartoon Network](/wiki/Cartoon_Network "Cartoon Network"), [E!](/wiki/E! "E!"), [Fox Sports 1](/wiki/Fox_Sports_1 "Fox Sports 1"), [Freeform](/wiki/Freeform_(TV_channel) "Freeform (TV channel)"), [FX](/wiki/FX_(TV_channel) "FX (TV channel)") and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN")).[[205]](#cite_note-bbc-yttv-206)[[206]](#cite_note-fortune-yttv-207)

[Subscribers](/wiki/Subscribers "Subscribers") can receive premium cable channels (including [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") (via a combined [Max](/wiki/Max_(streaming_service) "Max (streaming service)") add-on that includes in-app and log-in access to the service), [Cinemax](/wiki/Cinemax "Cinemax"), [Showtime](/wiki/Showtime_(TV_network) "Showtime (TV network)"), [Starz](/wiki/Starz "Starz") and [MGM+](/wiki/MGM%2B "MGM+")) and other subscription services (such as [NFL Sunday Ticket](/wiki/NFL_Sunday_Ticket "NFL Sunday Ticket"), [MLB.tv](/wiki/MLB.tv "MLB.tv"), [NBA League Pass](/wiki/NBA_League_Pass "NBA League Pass"), [Curiosity Stream](/wiki/Curiosity_Stream "Curiosity Stream") and [Fox Nation](/wiki/Fox_Nation "Fox Nation")) as optional add-ons for an extra fee, and can access [YouTube Premium](/wiki/YouTube_Premium "YouTube Premium") original content.[[205]](#cite_note-bbc-yttv-206)[[206]](#cite_note-fortune-yttv-207) In September 2022, YouTube TV began allowing customers to purchase most of its premium add-ons (excluding certain services such as NBA League Pass and [AMC+](/wiki/AMC%2B "AMC+")) without an existing subscription to its base package.[[207]](#cite_note-208)

### YouTube Go

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/YouTube_Go.svg/250px-YouTube_Go.svg.png)](/wiki/File:YouTube_Go.svg)

Logo of YouTube Go

In September 2016, YouTube Go was announced,[[208]](#cite_note-209) as an [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") app created for making YouTube easier to access on mobile devices in [emerging markets](/wiki/Emerging_market "Emerging market"). It was distinct from the company's main Android app and allowed videos to be downloaded and shared with other users. It also allowed users to preview videos, share downloaded videos through [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth"), and offered more options for mobile data control and [video resolution](/wiki/Display_resolution "Display resolution").[[209]](#cite_note-210)

In February 2017, YouTube Go was launched in [India](/wiki/India "India"), and expanded in November 2017 to 14 other countries, including [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa").[[210]](#cite_note-211)[[211]](#cite_note-212) On February 1, 2018, it was rolled out in 130 countries worldwide, including [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), and [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"). Before it shut down, the app was available to around 60% of the world's population.[[212]](#cite_note-213)[[213]](#cite_note-214) In May 2022, Google announced that they would be shutting down YouTube Go in August 2022.[[214]](#cite_note-215)

### YouTube Shorts

Main article: [YouTube Shorts](/wiki/YouTube_Shorts "YouTube Shorts")

An example video which is suitable for YouTube Shorts, showing [Crew Dragon *Endeavour*](/wiki/Crew_Dragon_Endeavour "Crew Dragon Endeavour") docking at the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station")

In September 2020, YouTube announced that it would be launching a beta version of a new platform of 15-second videos, similar to [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok"), called [YouTube Shorts](/wiki/YouTube_Shorts "YouTube Shorts").[[215]](#cite_note-216)[[216]](#cite_note-217) The platform was tested in India and later expanded to other countries, including the United States in March 2021, with videos allowed up to 1 minute long.[[217]](#cite_note-218) The platform is not a standalone app, but is integrated into the main YouTube app. Like TikTok, it gives users access to built-in creative tools, including the possibility of adding licensed music to their videos.[[218]](#cite_note-219) The platform had its global beta launch on July 13, 2021.[[219]](#cite_note-220) On October 15, 2024, the platform officially extended the length of shorts to 3 minutes.[[220]](#cite_note-221)

### YouTube Stories

In 2018, YouTube started testing a new feature initially called "YouTube Reels".[[221]](#cite_note-222) The feature was nearly identical to [Instagram Stories](/wiki/Instagram_Stories "Instagram Stories") and [Snapchat Stories](/wiki/Snapchat#Stories_and_Discover "Snapchat"). YouTube later renamed the feature "YouTube Stories". It was only available to creators who had more than 10,000 subscribers and could only be posted/seen in the YouTube mobile app.[[222]](#cite_note-223) On May 25, 2023, YouTube announced that they would be shutting down this feature on June 26, 2023.[[223]](#cite_note-224)[[224]](#cite_note-225)

### YouTube VR

Main article: [YouTube VR](/wiki/YouTube_VR "YouTube VR")

In November 2016, YouTube released [YouTube VR](/wiki/YouTube_VR "YouTube VR"), a dedicated version with an interface for VR devices, for [Google's Daydream](/wiki/Google_Daydream "Google Daydream") mobile VR platform on Android.[[225]](#cite_note-VR-226) In November 2018, YouTube VR was released on the [Oculus Store](/wiki/Meta_Horizon_Store "Meta Horizon Store") for the [Oculus Go](/wiki/Oculus_Go "Oculus Go") headset.[[225]](#cite_note-VR-226) YouTube VR was updated since for compatibility with successive [Quest](/wiki/Meta_Horizon_OS "Meta Horizon OS") devices, and was ported to [Pico 4](/wiki/Pico_4 "Pico 4").[[226]](#cite_note-227)

YouTube VR allows for access to all YouTube-hosted videos, but particularly supports headset access for 360° and 180°-degree video (both in 2D and stereoscopic 3D). Starting with the [Oculus Quest](/wiki/Oculus_Quest "Oculus Quest"), the app was updated for compatibility with mixed-reality passthrough modes on VR headsets. In April 2024, YouTube VR was updated to support 8K SDR video on [Meta Quest 3](/wiki/Meta_Quest_3 "Meta Quest 3").[[227]](#cite_note-228)

### Playables

In 2010, YouTube added *[Snake](/wiki/Snake_(video_game_genre) "Snake (video game genre)")* as a hidden game inside their [video player](/wiki/Video_player "Video player").[[228]](#cite_note-229) In May 2024, YouTube introduced Playables, a set of around 75 free-to-play games that can be played on the platform.[[229]](#cite_note-230)

### Automatic language dubbing

In December 2024, YouTube added the functionality of automatic language dubbing, which uses [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") to produce translations of videos into different languages.[[230]](#cite_note-231)[[231]](#cite_note-232)[[232]](#cite_note-233)[[233]](#cite_note-234) However, the feature has initially been criticized for providing robotic-sounding dubs, mistranslations, and lack of an option for the user to disable auto-dubbed voices.[[234]](#cite_note-235)[[235]](#cite_note-236)

## Criticism and controversies

This section is an excerpt from [YouTube moderation](/wiki/YouTube_moderation "YouTube moderation").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube_moderation&action=edit)]

YouTube, a video sharing platform, has faced various criticisms over the years, particularly regarding [content moderation](/wiki/Content_moderation "Content moderation"), offensive content, and monetization. YouTube has faced criticism over aspects of its operations,[[236]](#cite_note-YouTube_moderation_Alexander-237) its [recommendation algorithms](/wiki/Recommender_system "Recommender system") perpetuating [videos that promote conspiracy theories](#Promotion_of_conspiracy_theories_and_fringe_discourse) and falsehoods,[[237]](#cite_note-238) hosting videos ostensibly targeting children but containing [violent or sexually suggestive content involving popular characters](/wiki/Elsagate "Elsagate"),[[238]](#cite_note-239) videos of minors attracting [pedophilic](/wiki/Pedophilia "Pedophilia") activities in their comment sections,[[239]](#cite_note-240) and fluctuating policies on the types of content that is eligible to be monetized with advertising.[[236]](#cite_note-YouTube_moderation_Alexander-237)

YouTube has also been blocked by several countries. As of 2018, public access to YouTube was blocked by countries including [China](/wiki/China "China"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan"),[[240]](#cite_note-241) [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"),[[241]](#cite_note-242)[[242]](#cite_note-243) [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"), [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea"), [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan").

### Privacy concerns

This section is an excerpt from [YouTube and privacy](/wiki/YouTube_and_privacy "YouTube and privacy").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube_and_privacy&action=edit)]

Since its founding in 2005, the American video-sharing website YouTube has been faced with a growing number of privacy issues, including allegations that it allows users to upload unauthorized copyrighted material and allows personal information from young children to be collected without their parents' consent.

In September 2024, the [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") released a report summarizing 9 company responses (including from YouTube) to orders made by the agency pursuant to Section 6(b) of the [Federal Trade Commission Act of 1914](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission_Act_of_1914 "Federal Trade Commission Act of 1914") to provide information about user and non-user data collection (including of children and teenagers) and data use by the companies that found that the companies' user and non-user data practices put individuals vulnerable to [identity theft](/wiki/Identity_theft "Identity theft"), [stalking](/wiki/Stalking "Stalking"), unlawful discrimination, emotional distress and [mental health issues](/wiki/Digital_media_use_and_mental_health "Digital media use and mental health"), social stigma, and reputational harm.[[243]](#cite_note-244)[[244]](#cite_note-245)[[245]](#cite_note-246)

### Censorship and bans

Main article: [Censorship of YouTube](/wiki/Censorship_of_YouTube "Censorship of YouTube")

#### State censorship of YouTube content

YouTube has been censored, filtered, or banned for a variety of reasons, including:[[246]](#cite_note-ONIYouTubeCensored-247)

* Limiting public access and exposure to content that may ignite social or political unrest.
* Preventing criticism of a ruler (e.g. in [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea")), government (e.g. in [China](/wiki/Internet_censorship_in_China "Internet censorship in China")) or its actions (e.g. in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco")), government officials (e.g. in [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya")), or religion (e.g. in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")).
* Morality-based laws, e.g. in [Iran](/wiki/Internet_censorship_in_Iran "Internet censorship in Iran").

Access to specific videos is sometimes prevented due to copyright and intellectual property protection laws (e.g. [in Germany](/wiki/Blocking_of_YouTube_videos_in_Germany "Blocking of YouTube videos in Germany")), violations of hate speech, and preventing access to videos judged inappropriate for youth,[[247]](#cite_note-248) which is also done by YouTube with the [YouTube Kids](/wiki/YouTube_Kids "YouTube Kids") app and with "[restricted mode](/wiki/Censorship_by_Google#Censorship_of_sexual_content_in_Restricted_Mode "Censorship by Google")".[[248]](#cite_note-249) Businesses, schools, government agencies, and other private institutions often block social media sites, including YouTube, due to its bandwidth limitations[[249]](#cite_note-250)[[250]](#cite_note-251) and the site's potential for distraction.[[246]](#cite_note-ONIYouTubeCensored-247)[[251]](#cite_note-252)

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=YouTube&action=edit), public access to YouTube is blocked by [China](/wiki/China "China"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan"),[[252]](#cite_note-253) [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"),[[253]](#cite_note-254)[[254]](#cite_note-255) [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"), [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea"), [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") mostly due to freedom of speech laws. In some countries, YouTube is blocked for more limited periods of time, such as during periods of unrest, the run-up to an election, or in response to upcoming political anniversaries. In cases where the entire site is banned due to one particular video, YouTube will often agree to remove or limit access to that video to restore service.[[246]](#cite_note-ONIYouTubeCensored-247)

Reports emerged that since October 2019, comments posted with Chinese characters insulting the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party") ([共匪](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%B1%E5%8C%AA "wikt:共匪") "communist bandit" or [五毛](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%94%E6%AF%9B "wikt:五毛") "[50 Cent Party](/wiki/50_Cent_Party "50 Cent Party")", referring to [state-sponsored commentators](/wiki/State-sponsored_Internet_propaganda "State-sponsored Internet propaganda")) were being automatically deleted within 15 seconds.[[255]](#cite_note-256) Specific incidents where YouTube has been blocked include:

* [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") blocked access in April 2007 over a video said to be insulting the [Thai king](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Thailand "Monarchy of Thailand").[[256]](#cite_note-257)
* Morocco blocked access in May 2007, possibly as a result of videos critical of [Morocco's occupation of Western Sahara](/wiki/Political_status_of_Western_Sahara "Political status of Western Sahara").[[257]](#cite_note-258) YouTube became accessible again on May 30, 2007, after *Maroc Telecom* unofficially announced that the denied access to the website was a mere "technical glitch".[[258]](#cite_note-259)
* Turkey blocked access between 2008 and 2010 after controversy over videos deemed insulting to [Mustafa Kemal Atatürk](/wiki/Mustafa_Kemal_Atat%C3%BCrk "Mustafa Kemal Atatürk").[[259]](#cite_note-260)[[260]](#cite_note-261)[[261]](#cite_note-262) In November 2010, a video of the Turkish politician [Deniz Baykal](/wiki/Deniz_Baykal "Deniz Baykal") caused the site to be blocked again briefly, and the site was threatened with a new shutdown if it did not remove the video.[[262]](#cite_note-263) During the two and a half-year block of YouTube, the video-sharing website remained the eighth-most-accessed site in Turkey.[[263]](#cite_note-264)[[264]](#cite_note-265) In 2014, Turkey blocked the access for the second time, after "a high-level intelligence leak".[[265]](#cite_note-266)[[266]](#cite_note-267)[[267]](#cite_note-268)
* Pakistan blocked access on February 23, 2008, because of "offensive material" towards the Islamic faith, including display of the [Danish cartoons](/wiki/Jyllands-Posten_Muhammad_cartoons_controversy "Jyllands-Posten Muhammad cartoons controversy") of [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad").[[268]](#cite_note-269) This led to a near global blackout of the YouTube site for around two hours, as the Pakistani block was inadvertently transferred to other countries. On February 26, 2008, the ban was lifted after the website had removed the objectionable content from its servers at the request of the government.[[269]](#cite_note-270)[[270]](#cite_note-271) Many Pakistanis circumvented the three-day block by using [virtual private network](/wiki/Virtual_private_network "Virtual private network") software.[[271]](#cite_note-272) In May 2010, following the [Everybody Draw Mohammed Day](/wiki/Everybody_Draw_Mohammed_Day "Everybody Draw Mohammed Day"), Pakistan again blocked access to YouTube, citing "growing sacrilegious content".[[272]](#cite_note-273) The ban was lifted on May 27, 2010, after the website removed the objectionable content from its servers at the request of the government. However, individual videos deemed offensive to Muslims posted on YouTube will continue to be blocked.[[273]](#cite_note-274)[[274]](#cite_note-275) Pakistan again placed a ban on YouTube in September 2012, after the site refused to remove the film *[Innocence of Muslims](/wiki/Innocence_of_Muslims "Innocence of Muslims")*. The ban was lifted in January 2016 after YouTube launched a Pakistan-specific version.[[275]](#cite_note-276)
* Libya blocked access on January 24, 2010, because of videos that featured demonstrations in the city of [Benghazi](/wiki/Benghazi "Benghazi") by families of detainees who were [killed in Abu Salim prison in 1996](/wiki/Abu_Salim_Prison_massacre "Abu Salim Prison massacre"), and videos of family members of Libyan leader [Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi") at parties. The blocking was criticized by [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch").[[276]](#cite_note-277) In November 2011, after the [Libyan Civil War](/wiki/2011_Libyan_Civil_War "2011 Libyan Civil War"), YouTube was once again allowed in Libya.[[277]](#cite_note-278)
* [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), and [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") blocked access in September 2012 following [controversy](/wiki/Reactions_to_Innocence_of_Muslims "Reactions to Innocence of Muslims") over a 14-minute trailer for the film *[Innocence of Muslims](/wiki/Innocence_of_Muslims "Innocence of Muslims")* which had been posted on the site.[[278]](#cite_note-279)[[279]](#cite_note-280)[[280]](#cite_note-281)[[281]](#cite_note-282)[[282]](#cite_note-283) A court in the southern Russian Republic of [Chechnya](/wiki/Chechnya "Chechnya") ruled that *Innocence of Muslims* should be banned.[[283]](#cite_note-284) In Libya and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), it was blamed for violent protests. YouTube stated: "This video—which is widely available on the Web—is clearly within our guidelines and so will stay on YouTube. However, given the very difficult situation in Libya and Egypt, we have temporarily restricted access in both countries."[[284]](#cite_note-285)[[285]](#cite_note-286)

#### Bans by YouTube

Following U.S. government foreign policy decisions, YouTube has censored content posted by several organizations:

* Following the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine") in February 2022, YouTube announced on March 1 the immediate removal of [RT](/wiki/RT_(TV_network) "RT (TV network)") (and other Russian government-funded outlets) from its platform in Europe. The removal was soon expanded globally.[[286]](#cite_note-287) From late 2024, users across Russia started experiencing sharp declines in YouTube loading speeds.[[287]](#cite_note-288)
* YouTube removed the videos and deleted the accounts of three [Human Rights Organizations](/wiki/Human_rights_group "Human rights group") that document the human toll of the [Gaza War](/wiki/Gaza_War "Gaza War"). In early October 2025, complying with [Trump Administration](/wiki/Foreign_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Foreign policy of the second Trump administration") [sanctions](/wiki/International_sanctions "International sanctions") against three of the leading [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian "Palestinian") human rights groups, [Al-Haq](/wiki/Al-Haq "Al-Haq") based in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), the [Al Mezan Center for Human Rights](/wiki/Al_Mezan_Center_for_Human_Rights "Al Mezan Center for Human Rights") based in [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"), and the [Palestinian Centre for Human Rights](/wiki/Palestinian_Centre_for_Human_Rights "Palestinian Centre for Human Rights") also based in Gaza, YouTube removed 700 videos and the accounts of these organizations. The videos included an investigation uncovering Israel's role in the killing of a Palestinian American journalist [Shireen Abu Akleh](/wiki/Killing_of_Shireen_Abu_Akleh "Killing of Shireen Abu Akleh"), a video documenting Israel's destruction of Palestinian homes in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), a video of mothers in Gaza who survived Israel's attacks, and testimonies of [Palestinian detainees tortured in Israel](/wiki/Torture_during_the_Gaza_war "Torture during the Gaza war"). The Trump Administration has also sanctioned the [International Criminal Court](/wiki/International_Criminal_Court "International Criminal Court") (ICC), which has indicted senior Israeli officials for war crimes in Gaza, ICC court officials, and anyone accused of aiding their efforts.
