Earlier in 2025, YouTube shuttered the account and removed the videos of the [Addameer Prisoner Support and Human Rights Association](/wiki/Addameer_Prisoner_Support_and_Human_Rights_Association "Addameer Prisoner Support and Human Rights Association"), a Palestinian [Non-Government Organization](/wiki/Non-Government_Organization "Non-Government Organization") (NGO) that monitors the treatment of Palestinian detainees arrested in the West Bank.[[288]](#cite_note-289)

## Social impact

Main article: [Social impact of YouTube](/wiki/Social_impact_of_YouTube "Social impact of YouTube")

Private individuals,[[289]](#cite_note-Reuters20070225-290) as well as large production corporations,[[290]](#cite_note-WiredUK20131127-291) have used YouTube to grow their audiences. Indie creators have built grassroots followings numbering in the thousands at very little cost or effort, while mass retail and radio promotion proved problematic.[[289]](#cite_note-Reuters20070225-290) Concurrently, [old media](/wiki/Old_media "Old media") celebrities moved into the website at the invitation of a YouTube management that witnessed early content creators accruing substantial followings and perceived audience sizes potentially larger than that attainable by television.[[290]](#cite_note-WiredUK20131127-291) While YouTube's revenue-sharing "Partner Program" made it possible to earn a substantial living as a [video producer](/wiki/Video_production "Video production")—its top five hundred partners each earning more than $100,000 annually[[291]](#cite_note-292) and its ten highest-earning channels grossing from $2.5 million to $12 million[[292]](#cite_note-293)—in 2012 [CMU](/wiki/Complete_Music_Update "Complete Music Update") business editor characterized YouTube as "a free-to-use ... promotional platform for the music labels."[[293]](#cite_note-294) In 2013, Katheryn Thayer of *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* asserted that digital-era artists' work must not only be of high quality, but must elicit reactions on the YouTube platform and social media.[[294]](#cite_note-295) Videos of the 2.5% of artists categorized as "mega", "mainstream" and "mid-sized" received 90.3% of the relevant views on YouTube and Vevo in that year.[[295]](#cite_note-296) By early 2013, *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* had announced that it was factoring YouTube streaming data into calculation of the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") and related genre charts.[[296]](#cite_note-297)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Jordan_Hoffner_at_the_68th_Annual_Peabody_Awards_for_YouTube.jpg/250px-Jordan_Hoffner_at_the_68th_Annual_Peabody_Awards_for_YouTube.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jordan_Hoffner_at_the_68th_Annual_Peabody_Awards_for_YouTube.jpg)

Jordan Hoffner at the 68th Annual [Peabody Awards](/wiki/Peabody_Awards "Peabody Awards") accepting for YouTube

Observing that face-to-face communication of the type that online videos convey has been "fine-tuned by millions of years of evolution", [TED](/wiki/TED_(conference) "TED (conference)") curator [Chris Anderson](/wiki/Chris_Anderson_(entrepreneur) "Chris Anderson (entrepreneur)") referred to several YouTube contributors and asserted that "what [Gutenberg](/wiki/Johannes_Gutenberg "Johannes Gutenberg") did for writing, online video can now do for face-to-face communication."[[297]](#cite_note-TED201007Anderson-298) Anderson asserted that it is not far-fetched to say that online video will dramatically accelerate scientific advance, and that video contributors may be about to launch "the biggest learning cycle in human history."[[297]](#cite_note-TED201007Anderson-298) In education, for example, the [Khan Academy](/wiki/Khan_Academy "Khan Academy") grew from YouTube video tutoring sessions for founder Salman Khan's cousin into what *Forbes'* Michael Noer called "the largest school in the world", with technology poised to [disrupt](/wiki/Disruptive_innovation "Disruptive innovation") how people learn.[[298]](#cite_note-299) YouTube was awarded a 2008 [George Foster Peabody Award](/wiki/George_Foster_Peabody_Award "George Foster Peabody Award"),[[299]](#cite_note-300) the website being described as a [Speakers' Corner](/wiki/Speakers%27_Corner "Speakers' Corner") that "both embodies and promotes democracy".[[300]](#cite_note-301) *The Washington Post* reported that a disproportionate share of YouTube's most-subscribed channels feature minorities, contrasting with mainstream television in which the stars are largely white.[[301]](#cite_note-302) A [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") study reported the development of "visual journalism", in which citizen eyewitnesses and established news organizations share in content creation.[[302]](#cite_note-303) The study also concluded that YouTube was becoming an important platform by which people acquire news.[[303]](#cite_note-304)

YouTube has enabled people to more directly engage with government, such as in the [CNN/YouTube presidential debates](/wiki/CNN/YouTube_presidential_debates "CNN/YouTube presidential debates") (2007) in which ordinary people submitted questions to U.S. presidential candidates via YouTube video, with a [techPresident](/wiki/TechPresident "TechPresident") co-founder saying that Internet video was changing the political landscape.[[304]](#cite_note-305) Describing the [Arab Spring](/wiki/Arab_Spring "Arab Spring") (2010–2012), sociologist [Philip N. Howard](/wiki/Philip_N._Howard "Philip N. Howard") quoted an activist's succinct description that organizing the political unrest involved using "Facebook to schedule the protests, Twitter to coordinate, and YouTube to tell the world."[[305]](#cite_note-306) In 2012, more than a third of the U.S. Senate introduced a resolution condemning [Joseph Kony](/wiki/Joseph_Kony "Joseph Kony") 16 days after the "[Kony 2012](/wiki/Kony_2012 "Kony 2012")" video was posted to YouTube, with resolution co-sponsor Senator [Lindsey Graham](/wiki/Lindsey_Graham "Lindsey Graham") remarking that the video "will do more to lead to (Kony's) demise than all other action combined."[[306]](#cite_note-307)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/ObamaYouTubers307.png/330px-ObamaYouTubers307.png)](/wiki/File:ObamaYouTubers307.png)

Prominent YouTube content creators met at the White House with U.S. President Obama to discuss how government could better connect with the "YouTube generation".[[307]](#cite_note-Tubefilter20140302-308)[[308]](#cite_note-309)

Conversely, YouTube has also allowed government to more easily engage with citizens, the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House")'s official YouTube channel being the seventh top news organization producer on YouTube in 2012[[309]](#cite_note-310) and in 2013 a healthcare exchange commissioned Obama impersonator [Iman Crosson](/wiki/Iman_Crosson "Iman Crosson")'s YouTube music video spoof to encourage young Americans to enroll in the [Affordable Care Act (Obamacare)](/wiki/Patient_Protection_and_Affordable_Care_Act "Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act")-compliant health insurance.[[310]](#cite_note-311) In February 2014, U.S. President Obama held a meeting at the White House with leading YouTube content creators not only to promote awareness of Obamacare[[311]](#cite_note-312) but more generally to develop ways for government to better connect with the "YouTube Generation".[[307]](#cite_note-Tubefilter20140302-308) Whereas YouTube's inherent ability to allow presidents to directly connect with average citizens was noted, the YouTube content creators' [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media") savvy was perceived necessary to better cope with the website's distracting content and fickle audience.[[307]](#cite_note-Tubefilter20140302-308)

Some YouTube videos have themselves had a direct effect on world events, such as *[Innocence of Muslims](/wiki/Innocence_of_Muslims "Innocence of Muslims")* (2012) which spurred [protests and related anti-American violence](/wiki/Reactions_to_Innocence_of_Muslims "Reactions to Innocence of Muslims") internationally.[[312]](#cite_note-313) TED curator Chris Anderson described a phenomenon by which geographically distributed individuals in a certain field share their independently developed skills in YouTube videos, thus challenging others to improve their own skills, and spurring invention and evolution in that field.[[297]](#cite_note-TED201007Anderson-298) Journalist [Virginia Heffernan](/wiki/Virginia_Heffernan "Virginia Heffernan") stated in *The New York Times* that such videos have "surprising implications" for the dissemination of culture and even the future of classical music.[[313]](#cite_note-314)

A 2017 article in *[The New York Times Magazine](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Magazine "The New York Times Magazine")* posited that YouTube had become "the new [talk radio](/wiki/Conservative_talk_radio "Conservative talk radio")" for the [far right](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics").[[314]](#cite_note-315) Almost a year before YouTube's January 2019 announcement that it would begin a "gradual change" of "reducing [recommendations](/wiki/Recommender_system "Recommender system") of borderline content and content that could misinform users in harmful ways",[[315]](#cite_note-316) [Zeynep Tufekci](/wiki/Zeynep_Tufekci "Zeynep Tufekci") had written in *The New York Times* that, "(g)iven its billion or so users, YouTube may be one of the most powerful radicalizing instruments of the 21st century".[[316]](#cite_note-317) Under YouTube's changes to its recommendation engine, the most-recommended channel evolved from conspiracy theorist [Alex Jones](/wiki/Alex_Jones "Alex Jones") (2016) to [Fox News](/wiki/Fox_News "Fox News") (2019).[[317]](#cite_note-318) According to a 2020 study, viewership of far-right videos on YouTube peaked in 2017 and "a growing body of journalistic evidence" suggested that YouTube was radicalizing young men through its recommendation engine, but that such evidence was "fraught with a bias towards sensationalism". It also found more "mainstream-adjacent Conservative creators" gaining over [alt-right](/wiki/Alt-right "Alt-right") and extremist videos by 2020.[[318]](#cite_note-319) A 2022 study found that "despite widespread concerns that YouTube's algorithms send people down 'rabbit holes' with recommendations to extremist videos, little systematic evidence exists to support this conjecture", and that such exposure was "heavily concentrated among a small group of people with high prior levels of gender and racial resentment."[[319]](#cite_note-320) A 2024 study by the [Institute for Strategic Dialogue](/wiki/Institute_for_Strategic_Dialogue "Institute for Strategic Dialogue") found that YouTube frequently recommended Christian videos and right-leaning and culturally conservative "[culture war](/wiki/Culture_war "Culture war")" videos by Fox News and male lifestyle [influencers](/wiki/Influencers "Influencers") to accounts that did not show an interest in such topics.[[320]](#cite_note-321)

*[The Legion of Extraordinary Dancers](/wiki/The_Legion_of_Extraordinary_Dancers "The Legion of Extraordinary Dancers")*[[321]](#cite_note-322) and the [YouTube Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/YouTube_Symphony_Orchestra "YouTube Symphony Orchestra")[[322]](#cite_note-Reuters20090414-323) selected their membership based on individual video performances.[[297]](#cite_note-TED201007Anderson-298)[[322]](#cite_note-Reuters20090414-323) Further, the cyber-collaboration charity video "[We Are the World 25 for Haiti (YouTube edition)](/wiki/We_Are_the_World_25_for_Haiti_(YouTube_edition) "We Are the World 25 for Haiti (YouTube edition)")" was formed by mixing performances of 57 globally distributed singers into a single musical work,[[323]](#cite_note-324) with *The Tokyo Times* noting the "We Pray for You" YouTube cyber-collaboration video as an example of a trend to use [crowdsourcing](/wiki/Crowdsourcing "Crowdsourcing") for charitable purposes.[[324]](#cite_note-325)
The anti-bullying [It Gets Better Project](/wiki/It_Gets_Better_Project "It Gets Better Project") expanded from a single YouTube video directed to discouraged or [suicidal LGBT teens](/wiki/Suicide_among_LGBT_youth "Suicide among LGBT youth"),[[325]](#cite_note-326) that within two months drew video responses from hundreds including U.S. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), Vice President Biden, White House staff, and several cabinet secretaries.[[326]](#cite_note-327) Similarly, in response to fifteen-year-old [Amanda Todd](/wiki/Suicide_of_Amanda_Todd "Suicide of Amanda Todd")'s video "My story: Struggling, bullying, suicide, self-harm", legislative action was undertaken almost immediately after her suicide to study the prevalence of bullying and form a national anti-bullying strategy.[[327]](#cite_note-328) In May 2018, after London [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") claimed that [drill music](/wiki/UK_drill "UK drill") videos glamorizing violence gave rise to [gang violence](/wiki/Gang#Gang_violence "Gang"), YouTube deleted 30 videos.[[328]](#cite_note-329)

## Finances

Prior to 2020, Google did not provide detailed figures for YouTube's running costs, and YouTube's revenues in 2007 were noted as "[not material](/wiki/Materiality_(auditing) "Materiality (auditing)")" in a regulatory filing.[[329]](#cite_note-330) In June 2008, a *Forbes* magazine article projected the 2008 revenue at $200 million, noting progress in advertising sales.[[330]](#cite_note-331) In 2012, YouTube's revenue from its ads program was estimated at $3.7 billion.[[331]](#cite_note-Winkler-332) In 2013, it nearly doubled and estimated to hit $5.6 billion according to [e-Marketer](/wiki/Emarketer "Emarketer"),[[331]](#cite_note-Winkler-332)[[332]](#cite_note-333) while others estimated $4.7 billion.[[331]](#cite_note-Winkler-332) The vast majority of videos on YouTube are free to view and supported by advertising.[[66]](#cite_note-subscription-66) In May 2013, YouTube introduced a trial scheme of 53 subscription channels with prices ranging from $0.99 to $6.99 a month.[[333]](#cite_note-334) The move was seen as an attempt to compete with other providers of online subscription services such as [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"), [Amazon Prime](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video"), and [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu").[[66]](#cite_note-subscription-66)

Google first published exact revenue numbers for YouTube in February 2020 as part of Alphabet's 2019 financial report. According to Google, YouTube had made US$15.1 billion in ad revenue in 2019, in contrast to US$8.1 billion in 2017 and US$11.1 billion in 2018. YouTube's revenues made up nearly 10% of the total Alphabet revenue in 2019.[[334]](#cite_note-335)[[335]](#cite_note-336) These revenues accounted for approximately 20 million subscribers combined between YouTube Premium and YouTube Music subscriptions, and 2 million subscribers to [YouTube TV](/wiki/YouTube_TV "YouTube TV").[[336]](#cite_note-337) YouTube had $29.2 billion ads revenue in 2022, up by $398 million from the prior year.[[337]](#cite_note-338) In Q2 2024, ad revenue rose to $8.66 billion, up 13% on Q1.[[338]](#cite_note-339)

### Partnership with corporations

YouTube entered into a marketing and advertising partnership with [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") in June 2006.[[339]](#cite_note-340) In March 2007, it struck a deal with the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") for three channels with BBC content, one for news and two for entertainment.[[340]](#cite_note-341) In November 2008, YouTube reached an agreement with [MGM](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"), [Lions Gate Entertainment](/wiki/Lions_Gate_Entertainment "Lions Gate Entertainment"), and [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), allowing the companies to post full-length films and television episodes on the site, accompanied by advertisements in a section for U.S. viewers called "Shows". The move was intended to create competition with websites such as Hulu, which features material from NBC, [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company"), and [Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_Motion_Pictures "Walt Disney Studios Motion Pictures").[[341]](#cite_note-342)[[342]](#cite_note-343) In November 2009, YouTube launched a version of "Shows" available to UK viewers, offering around 4,000 full-length shows from more than 60 partners.[[343]](#cite_note-344) In January 2010, YouTube introduced a film rentals service,[[344]](#cite_note-345) available in many countries, and TV shows can be bought in several countries.[[345]](#cite_note-346)[[346]](#cite_note-347) The service offers over 6,000 films.[[347]](#cite_note-348)

#### 2017 advertiser boycott

In March 2017, the government of the United Kingdom pulled its advertising campaigns from YouTube, after reports that its ads had appeared on videos containing extremist content. The government demanded assurances that its advertising would "be delivered safely and appropriately". *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* newspaper, as well as other major British and U.S. brands, similarly suspended their advertising on YouTube in response to their advertising appearing near [offensive content](/wiki/Offensive_content "Offensive content"). Google stated that it had "begun an extensive review of our advertising policies and have made a public commitment to put in place changes that give brands more control over where their ads appear".[[348]](#cite_note-349)[[349]](#cite_note-350)

In early April 2017, the YouTube channel [h3h3Productions](/wiki/H3h3Productions "H3h3Productions") presented evidence claiming that a *[Wall Street Journal](/wiki/Wall_Street_Journal "Wall Street Journal")* article had fabricated screenshots showing major brand advertising on an offensive video containing [Johnny Rebel](/wiki/Johnny_Rebel_(singer) "Johnny Rebel (singer)") music overlaid on a [Chief Keef](/wiki/Chief_Keef "Chief Keef") music video, citing that the video itself had not earned any ad revenue for the uploader. The video was retracted after it was found that the ads had been triggered by the use of copyrighted content in the video.[[350]](#cite_note-351)[[351]](#cite_note-352) On April 6, 2017, YouTube announced that to "ensure revenue only flows to creators who are playing by the rules", it would change its practices to require that a channel undergo a policy compliance review, and have at least 10,000-lifetime views, before they may join the YouTube Partner Program.[[352]](#cite_note-353)

### YouTuber earnings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/2017-_Top_earners_on_YouTube_-_column_chart.svg/330px-2017-_Top_earners_on_YouTube_-_column_chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2017-_Top_earners_on_YouTube_-_column_chart.svg)

Total annual earnings of the top ten YouTuber accounts, and the income of the single highest-earning account for 2017

In May 2007, YouTube launched its Partner Program (YPP), a system based on [AdSense](/wiki/Google_AdSense "Google AdSense") which allows the uploader of the video to share the revenue produced by advertising on the site.[[353]](#cite_note-354) YouTube typically takes 45 percent of the advertising revenue from videos in the Partner Program, with 55 percent going to the uploader.[[354]](#cite_note-355)[[355]](#cite_note-356) There are over two million members of the YouTube Partner Program.[[356]](#cite_note-357) According to [TubeMogul](/wiki/TubeMogul "TubeMogul"), in 2013 a pre-roll advertisement on YouTube (one that is shown before the video starts) cost advertisers on average $7.60 per 1000 views. Usually, no more than half of the eligible videos have a pre-roll advertisement, due to a lack of interested advertisers.[[357]](#cite_note-358)

YouTube's policies restrict certain forms of content from being included in videos being monetized with advertising, including videos containing violence, strong language, sexual content, "controversial or sensitive subjects and events, including subjects related to war, political conflicts, natural disasters and tragedies, even if graphic imagery is not shown" (unless the content is "usually newsworthy or comedic and the creator intends to inform or entertain"),[[358]](#cite_note-Robertson-359) and videos whose user comments contain "inappropriate" content.[[359]](#cite_note-360)

In 2013, YouTube introduced an option for channels with at least a thousand subscribers to require a paid subscription for viewers to watch videos.[[360]](#cite_note-361)[[361]](#cite_note-362) In April 2017, YouTube set an eligibility requirement of 10,000 lifetime views for a paid subscription.[[362]](#cite_note-ypp-363) On January 16, 2018, the eligibility requirement for monetization was changed to 4,000 hours of watch-time within the past 12 months and 1,000 subscribers.[[362]](#cite_note-ypp-363) The move was seen as an attempt to ensure that videos being monetized did not lead to controversy, but was criticized for penalizing smaller YouTube channels.[[363]](#cite_note-364) [YouTube Play Buttons](/wiki/YouTube_Play_Button "YouTube Play Button"), a part of the YouTube Creator Rewards, are a recognition by YouTube of its most popular channels.[[364]](#cite_note-365) The trophies are made of nickel-plated copper-nickel alloy, golden-plated brass, silver-plated metal, ruby, and red-tinted crystal glass. They are given to channels with at least one hundred thousand, a million, ten million, fifty million, and one hundred million subscribers, respectively.[[365]](#cite_note-366)[[366]](#cite_note-367)

YouTube's policies on "[advertiser-friendly content](/wiki/Censorship_by_Google#Advertiser-friendly_content "Censorship by Google")" restrict what may be incorporated into videos being monetized; this includes strong violence, language,[[367]](#cite_note-368) sexual content, and "controversial or sensitive subjects and events, including subjects related to war, political conflicts, natural disasters and tragedies, even if graphic imagery is not shown", unless the content is "usually newsworthy or comedic and the creator's intent is to inform or entertain".[[358]](#cite_note-Robertson-359) In September 2016, after introducing an enhanced notification system to inform users of these violations, YouTube's policies were criticized by prominent users, including [Philip DeFranco](/wiki/Philip_DeFranco "Philip DeFranco") and [Vlogbrothers](/wiki/Vlogbrothers "Vlogbrothers"). DeFranco argued that not being able to earn advertising revenue on such videos was "censorship by a different name". A YouTube spokesperson stated that while the policy itself was not new, the service had "improved the notification and appeal process to ensure better communication to our creators".[[368]](#cite_note-369)[[369]](#cite_note-370)[[370]](#cite_note-371) *[Boing Boing](/wiki/Boing_Boing "Boing Boing")* reported in 2019 that LGBT keywords resulted in demonetization.[[371]](#cite_note-372) In the United States as of November 2020, and June 2021 worldwide,[[372]](#cite_note-373) YouTube reserves the right to monetize any video on the platform, even if their uploader is not a member of the YouTube Partner Program. This will occur on channels whose content is deemed "advertiser-friendly", and all revenue will go directly to Google without any share given to the uploader.[[373]](#cite_note-374)

### Revenue to copyright holders

The majority of YouTube's advertising revenue goes to the publishers and video producers who hold the rights to their videos; the company retains 45% of the ad revenue.[[374]](#cite_note-375) In 2010, it was reported that nearly a third of the videos with advertisements were uploaded without permission from the copyright holders. YouTube gives an option for copyright holders to locate and remove their videos or to have them continue running for revenue.[[375]](#cite_note-376) In May 2013, [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") began enforcing its copyright ownership and claiming the advertising revenue from video creators who posted screenshots of its games.[[376]](#cite_note-377) In February 2015, Nintendo agreed to share the revenue with the video creators through the Nintendo Creators Program.[[377]](#cite_note-378)[[378]](#cite_note-379)[[379]](#cite_note-380) On March 20, 2019, Nintendo announced on Twitter that the company will end the Creators program. Operations for the program ceased on March 20, 2019.[[380]](#cite_note-381)[[381]](#cite_note-382)
